Q1 — Place: My uncle's dinner table on Sunday afternoons. Six adults arguing across each other in three different languages, my grandmother quietly destroying everyone's logic from the head of the table with a single one-liner, my cousins bouncing under the table trying not to laugh. The food was always too much. There was always a moment around dessert when someone said something so absurd that the whole room broke. I lived for that breaking moment. I used to position my chair to get a clear sightline to my uncle so I could watch his face the half-second before he laughed.
Q2 — Habit: I do voices. Not impressions — voices. I'll be retelling a story about my coworker and I'll automatically slip into her exact speech rhythm, the way her vowels stretch when she's annoyed, the tiny pause she takes before she says 'okay so.' My partner says I have hundreds of these in my head and I deploy them without warning. I also collect overheard absurdities — small moments where a person says something that, taken out of context, becomes accidentally profound or accidentally ridiculous. I have a notes file. It's mostly two-sentence pairs.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain why a story makes someone laugh. The thing nobody understands is that the laugh isn't about the funny line — it's about the SETUP being slightly longer than the listener expected. You build a small mental tension by drifting deeper into a normal-seeming narrative. The listener's brain is filing details, predicting where this is going. Then you snap the frame. The laugh is the sound of their prediction being wrong in a way that flattered them — they were close enough to the right track that the reveal feels earned. If you reveal too early, no laugh. If you delay too long, no laugh. The whole thing is timing.
Q4 — Conflict: I had a bad falling-out with a guy I dated in my late 20s about whether my sense of humor was 'too much.' He told me at a dinner with his friends that I was being exhausting — that I made everything into a bit. The surface story was him asking me to tone it down. The real story was that I'd noticed his entire friend group was using a specific kind of dry irony as a stand-in for actual feeling, and my warmth was breaking their performance. He couldn't say that. He just told me to be quieter. I tried for about three weeks. I felt like I'd been put on mute.
Q5 — Sound: The exact two-beat silence after a really good line lands but before the laugh starts. It's not actually silence — there's this collective intake of breath, like the whole room is loading. If you're the one who said the line, that two-beat is the most exposed feeling in the world. If they don't laugh, you've miscalculated. If they laugh, you've earned something. I have whole years of my life organized around chasing that two-beat pause. I can hear it from across a room — someone else's audience tipping into it.
Q6 — Body: I was at a work meeting and I felt my body move into 'set up' position before my mind knew I had a joke ready. My shoulders shifted, my breath dropped half an inch lower in my chest, and I sat very still. About three seconds later the joke arrived in my head fully formed. My body had assembled it before my conscious mind had access. I delivered it. The whole room laughed. I have no memory of constructing the joke — I just remember my body announcing one was coming.
Q7 — Unworded: The specific feeling of a moment LANDING. Not just a joke landing — any moment. A perfect sentence at the right time, a perfect pause, a perfect look at the right person. When it lands, the room briefly synchronizes around it. You can feel everyone's attention click into the same shape for half a second. It's almost a physical sensation in the air. I've spent my life trying to engineer that click, and I've never been able to explain to anyone exactly what I'm chasing. It feels closer to playing music than to being funny.
